hero.tagline: Standard $10,000 cap and simplified procedures for quick resolution.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: Superior Court of the District of Columbia, Small Claims and Conciliation Branch

at_a_glance.label: Lawyers at trial
at_a_glance.detail: Permitted but not required; businesses must have a lawyer

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Must file a motion for a new trial within 3 days

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the small claims limit. You must either waive the excess amount or file in the Civil Division.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: The statute of limitations for written contracts is 3 years in D.C. Ensure you file within this period to pursue your claim.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, and they failed to repay as promised.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $5,000 to a colleague with the understanding it would be repaid in six months, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide documentation of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $8,000 over a year but stopped making payments after two months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $3,500, invoiced the client, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Include the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $6,000 on their account with your business and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $9,000 in unpaid rent over several months and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A client paid a $2,500 invoice with a check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Provide the bounced check, bank notice, and any communication with the issuer.

claims.name: Co-signer liability for defaulted loan
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now the lender is seeking payment from you.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement and any correspondence from the lender.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: D.C. provides a 3-year statute of limitations for most consumer disputes. Ensure you file within this period to seek redress.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to replace or repair it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Provide the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $500 but left your home in poor condition, and they refuse to refund or redo the job.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Include the service agreement, photos of the work, and correspondence.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and now refuses to refund or correct the work.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices, diagnostic reports, and any related communication.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory contractor work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $9,500 for home renovations that were left incomplete and poorly done.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from completion date. Present the contract, payment records, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You hired a caterer for $3,000 who didn't show up on the event day and refuses to refund your payment.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from event date. Include the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your account for several months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from first unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $8,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Present the sales contract, vehicle history, and repair estimates.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A retailer advertised a product as 'new' for $1,500, but it was actually refurbished, and they refuse to refund.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Include the advertisement, purchase receipt, and any correspondence.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: D.C. allows a 3-year statute of limitations for property damage claims. File promptly to seek compensation.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $4,000 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Provide police reports, repair estimates, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damages, and they refuse to cover the costs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Include photos, repair estimates, and any communication.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $7,000 worth of damage to your rental unit beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Present the lease agreement, move-in/out inspections, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $3,000 watch with a jeweler for repair, and they returned it with scratches and refuse to compensate.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from retrieval. Include service agreements, photos, and any related communication.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes (money only)
claim_categories.blurb: D.C. provides a 3-year statute of limitations for monetary landlord-tenant disputes. Eviction cases are handled separately.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $2,000 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and any correspondence.

claims.name: Security deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $1,500 from your deposit without specifying the reasons.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from lease termination. Include the lease agreement and any communication.

claims.name: Rent paid for uninhabitable conditions
claims.example: You paid $3,000 in rent while your apartment had no heat for two months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from issue. Provide photos, repair requests, and rent payment records.

claims.name: Landlord retained personal property after move-out
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your $1,200 bicycle and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from move-out. Include move-out documentation and any communication.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your $5,000 worth of belongings.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from eviction. Provide lease agreement, communication, and police reports if applicable.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent after early termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent totaling $4,000, terminated early, and the landlord refuses to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from termination. Include lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent owed
claims.example: Your tenant owes $9,000 in unpaid rent over several months and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent without proper notice
claims.example: Your tenant deducted $1,500 from rent for repairs they made without notifying you.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from deduction. Provide lease agreement and communication records.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: D.C. enforces a 3-year statute of limitations for wage-related claims. Timely filing is crucial to recover owed wages.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't issued payment.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Provide timesheets and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to pay your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Include resignation letter and payroll records.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer hasn't paid them.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Provide sales records and commission agreements.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime worth $800, but your employer paid only regular wages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Include timesheets and payroll records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: D.C. allows a 3-year statute of limitations for personal claims. Ensure timely filing to seek redress.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in $2,500 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend your $1,200 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Include proof of ownership and communication records.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $3,000 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft date. Provide police reports and proof of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: D.C. enforces a 3-year statute of limitations for vehicle-related claims. Prompt action is essential.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and now refuses to refund or correct the work.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices, diagnostic reports, and any related communication.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you had to pay $500 to retrieve it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from tow date. Include tow receipts, photos of the parking spot, and any communication.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled separately in the Landlord and Tenant Branch of the Superior Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Landlord and Tenant Branch of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Cases involving disputes over the title to real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of the Small Claims Branch.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Civil Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Defamation cases typically involve complex legal issues and are not suitable for small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Civil Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limit and complexity suitable for small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Civil Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are handled by specialized divisions of the court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Family Court or Probate Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Claims against the federal government are outside the jurisdiction of the D.C. Superior Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia

exclusions.explanation: Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Civil Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Civil Division of the Superior Court

exclusions.explanation: Workers' compensation claims are handled by the D.C. Department of Employment Services.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not suitable for small claims court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Arbitration as specified in the contract

exclusions.explanation: Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Probate Division of the Superior Court

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount plus service charge up to $25, or 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases where statutes provide for fee-shifting, such as consumer protection violations.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims and Conciliation Branch of the Superior Court of the District of Columbia
where_to_file.parent_court: Superior Court of the District of Columbia

filing_options: District where the defendant resides or where the cause of action arose
Any district where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing is not currently available for small claims cases in D.C.

service_fees.notes: Service by certified mail handled by the clerk's office.
Fees depend on the process server's rates.

forms.name: Application to Proceed Without Prepayment of Costs

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

other_fees.name: Jury demand
other_fees.notes: Fee for requesting a jury trial.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.notes: Ensure to file within 3 years from the date the contract was breached.
Oral agreements must be enforced within 3 years of the breach.
Claims related to the sale of goods have a 4-year limitation period.
File within 3 years from the date of default on the note.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge
entries.notes: Claims must be filed within 3 years from the last activity on the account.
Personal injury claims have a 3-year statute of limitations.
File within 3 years from the date the property was damaged.
The clock starts when the fraud is discovered or reasonably should have been discovered.
Claims must be filed within 3 years from the date the check was dishonored.
File within 3 years from the date the wages were due.
Claims related to security deposits must be filed within 3 years from lease termination.
Judgments can be enforced for up to 12 years from the date of entry.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the limitation period when the fraud is discovered or reasonably should have been discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of the jurisdiction, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can facilitate settlement and demonstrate good faith before filing.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: For unpaid wage claims, a written demand is recommended to document the employer's refusal to pay.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages when withholding a security deposit; failure to do so may forfeit their right to retain any portion.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Under the Consumer Protection Procedures Act, consumers may be entitled to treble damages and attorney's fees for violations.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and the amount sought.

forms.name: Information Sheet
forms.description: Provides additional details about the parties involved in the case.

forms.name: Application to Proceed Without Prepayment of Costs
forms.description: Requests a waiver of filing and service fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Praecipe
forms.description: Notifies the court of actions such as settlement or dismissal.

forms.name: Subpoena
forms.description: Commands a witness to appear in court or produce documents.

forms.name: Writ of Execution
forms.description: Authorizes the seizure of a debtor's property to satisfy a judgment.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Initiates an appeal of the small claims court's decision.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: The clerk sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified mail.
cons: May be less reliable if defendant refuses delivery

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with D.C. rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow service by publication as a last resort.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer; they can present their defense orally at the hearing.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim amount.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion for a new trial must be filed within 3 days of judgment to appeal a default judgment.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to the Civil Division.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required; businesses must have legal representation.
hearing.format: Bench trial with simplified procedures; each side presents their case, and the judge makes a decision.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter through the court's Language Access Program prior to the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Mediation services are available to help parties reach a settlement before trial.

appeals.type_notes: The case is retried as if it were new, with no deference to the small claims court's decision.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A motion for a new trial must be filed within 3 days to appeal a default judgment.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at a rate of 6% per annum.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of attachment, serve the bank, and the bank freezes the debtor's account up to the judgment amount.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property, preventing its sale until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.description: File a judgment lien with the Recorder of Deeds, which attaches to the debtor's real property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, have the U.S. Marshal seize the debtor's property, and sell it at public auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 12-year period expires; no additional fee required.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses must be represented by an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must use their legal entity name when filing or defending a claim.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may represent themselves but cannot send an employee in their place.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for services requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations expires leads to automatic dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed period.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay the case or lead to dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process precisely, using approved methods.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: Defendants may file counterclaims that could offset or exceed your claim.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to address potential counterclaims and gather evidence to support your position.

pitfalls.title: Inadequate evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of sufficient evidence can result in losing the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Collect and organize all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring post-judgment procedures
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; failure to follow up can leave you unpaid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Learn about and utilize post-judgment collection methods to enforce the judgment.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims jurisdictional limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims cases increased from $5,000 to $10,000, allowing more cases to be heard in the Small Claims Branch.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, individuals are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court; however, businesses must be represented by an attorney.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases typically resolve within 30 to 90 days, depending on the complexity and court schedule.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in D.C. small claims court is $10,000, excluding interest, attorney fees, and costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims decision by filing a motion for a new trial within 3 days of the judgment.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in D.C. is enforceable for 12 years from the date of entry and can be renewed before expiration.

state_specific_notes: D.C. small claims court has a jurisdictional limit of $10,000, excluding interest and costs.
Businesses must be represented by an attorney in small claims court.
The statute of limitations for most claims in D.C. is 3 years.
Eviction cases are handled separately in the Landlord and Tenant Branch.
D.C. allows for jury trials in small claims cases upon request.

interesting_findings: D.C. small claims court offers on-site mediation services to help parties settle disputes before trial.
The court provides free interpreter services upon request to ensure language access for all parties.
Filing fees in D.C. small claims court are relatively low, with a flat fee of $45 for all claims up to $10,000.
D.C. small claims court procedures are designed to be accessible to individuals without legal representation.
The increase in the jurisdictional limit to $10,000 in 2017 expanded access to the small claims process for more litigants.
